content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 11, 1993

Mr. Randall J. Zakreski
Boehringer Ingelheim Corporation
P.O. Box 368
Ridgefield, Connecticut 06877

RE: Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No. I-93-296

Dear Mr. Zakreski:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice on behalf
of Boehringer Ingelheim Pharmaceuticals, Inc. ("BIPI") concerning
the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/
Your letter states only general questions. Therefore, we consider
it to be a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c).2/

Your letter accurately summarizes the advice that I provided
to you concerning special election limits. The term special
election means an election which is called to fill a vacant
position. Therefore, under state law, corporations are limited to
$1,000 per candidate per election cycle when contributing to
candidates involved in a special election. (Section 85305.) In
addition, there are some cities and counties that have imposed
contribution limits and other campaign requirements additional to
or different from those contained in the Act. These additional or
different requirements may only be imposed on local officeholders,
local candidates, committees formed to support or oppose local
candidates and ballot measures being voted upon in that
jurisdiction and to general purpose committees active only in the
local jurisdiction. (Section 81009.5.) The Act no longer limits
how much money a corporation may contribute to officeholders or
candidates during regular elections. (Section 85301.)

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Randall J. Zakreski
Our File No. I-93-296
Page 2




A corporation which makes contributions totaling $10,000 or
more in a calendar year to any number of California state or local
candidates or committees will qualify as a major donor committee
and be required to file disclosure statements. Section 83013(c)
defines the term "major donor committee" as any person or
combination of persons that makes contributions totaling $10,000
or more in a calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or
committees.

If you have any questions concerning this letter, please call
me at 916/322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Mary Ann Kvasager
                                        Political Reform Consultant

sections.question_synthetic: What are the campaign contribution limits for corporations under California's Political Reform Act, particularly regarding special elections, and what are the requirements for major donor committee disclosure?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Corporations are limited to $1,000 per candidate per election cycle for special elections (elections to fill vacant positions), but have no limits for regular elections under state law. Corporations making $10,000 or more in contributions to California candidates or committees in a calendar year must register as major donor committees and file disclosure statements. Local jurisdictions may impose additional contribution limits on local candidates and measures.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter confirming prior telephone advice; no formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections but substantive advice clearly provided; document structure indicates this is response to request for confirmation of informal telephone assistance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the campaign contribution limits for corporations under California's Political Reform Act, particularly regarding special elections, and what are the requirements for major donor committee disclosure?

CONCLUSION: Corporations are limited to $1,000 per candidate per election cycle for special elections (elections to fill vacant positions), but have no limits for regular elections under state law. Corporations making $10,000 or more in contributions to California candidates or committees in a calendar year must register as major donor committees and file disclosure statements. Local jurisdictions may impose additional contribution limits on local candidates and measures.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides informal written advice confirming telephone guidance given to Boehringer Ingelheim Pharmaceuticals regarding corporate campaign contribution limits under the Political Reform Act, including special election limits, regular election limits, and major donor committee disclosure requirements.
